---
title: "14 ┬À Policy Iteration"
subtitle: "Alternate exact policy evaluation with greedy improvement; converges in finitely many steps."
lesson-id: "mfrl.policy-iteration"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["mfrl.value-iteration"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "done"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you state the [Bellman equation](/glossary.qmd#bellman-equation) for
   $v_\pi$ (linear, with $\mathbb{E}_\pi$, no $\max$)?
2. Can you compute $v_\pi$ for a small MDP by either iteration or the
   matrix formula $v_\pi = (I - \gamma P_\pi)^{-1} r_\pi$?
3. Are you comfortable extracting a greedy policy from a value function?

If shaky, revisit Lessons 6 (Bellman equation), 7 (matrix form), and 13 (VI).
:::

## Touch

Policy iteration alternates two boxes. Imagine them as a loop:

```
        ÔöîÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÉ
        Ôû╝                        Ôöé
  ÔöîÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÉ         ÔöîÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÉ
  Ôöé  Evaluate    Ôöé         Ôöé   Improve    Ôöé
  Ôöé   v_¤Ç for    Ôöé  ÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔû║  Ôöé  ¤Ç greedy    Ôöé
  Ôöé  fixed ¤Ç     Ôöé         Ôöé  w.r.t. v_¤Ç  Ôöé
  ÔööÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÿ         ÔööÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÿ
        Ôû▓                        Ôöé
        ÔööÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÿ
```

Below is a 3├ù3 grid (same as Lesson 13). Slide to step through the PI
iterations. Each iteration shows two snapshots: $v_{\pi_k}$ (after exact
evaluation) and the new policy $\pi_{k+1}$ (after greedy improvement).

```{ojs}
viewof piIter = Inputs.range([0, 6], {value: 0, step: 1, label: "PI iteration k"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const N = 3;
  const goal = [2, 2];
  const gamma = 0.9;
  const next = (i, j, a) => {
    if (i === goal[0] && j === goal[1]) return [i, j];
    let ni = i, nj = j;
    if (a === 0 && i > 0) ni = i - 1;
    if (a === 1 && i < N - 1) ni = i + 1;
    if (a === 2 && j > 0) nj = j - 1;
    if (a === 3 && j < N - 1) nj = j + 1;
    return [ni, nj];
  };
  const reward = (i, j) => (i === goal[0] && j === goal[1] ? 1 : 0);
  const flat = (i, j) => i * N + j;

  // Start with policy "always up" ÔÇö deliberately bad
  let policy = Array.from({length: N}, () => Array(N).fill(0));

  // Solve v_¤Ç by iterative evaluation (50 inner sweeps)
  function evalPolicy(policy) {
    let v = Array.from({length: N}, () => Array(N).fill(0));
    for (let it = 0; it < 200; it++) {
      const vn = Array.from({length: N}, () => Array(N).fill(0));
      for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) for (let j = 0; j < N; j++) {
        const a = policy[i][j];
        const [ni, nj] = next(i, j, a);
        vn[i][j] = reward(ni, nj) + gamma * v[ni][nj];
      }
      v = vn;
    }
    return v;
  }
  function improve(v) {
    const p = Array.from({length: N}, () => Array(N).fill(0));
    for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) for (let j = 0; j < N; j++) {
      let best = -Infinity, ba = 0;
      for (let a = 0; a < 4; a++) {
        const [ni, nj] = next(i, j, a);
        const q = reward(ni, nj) + gamma * v[ni][nj];
        if (q > best) { best = q; ba = a; }
      }
      p[i][j] = ba;
    }
    return p;
  }

  let v = evalPolicy(policy);
  for (let k = 0; k < piIter; k++) {
    policy = improve(v);
    v = evalPolicy(policy);
  }

  const arrow = a => ["Ôåæ", "Ôåô", "ÔåÉ", "ÔåÆ"][a];
  const cells = [];
  let vmax = 0.001;
  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) for (let j = 0; j < N; j++) vmax = Math.max(vmax, Math.abs(v[i][j]));
  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {
    for (let j = 0; j < N; j++) {
      const intensity = Math.round((Math.abs(v[i][j]) / vmax) * 200);
      const colour = `rgb(${255 - intensity}, ${255 - intensity / 2}, 255)`;
      const sym = (i === goal[0] && j === goal[1]) ? "Ôÿà" : arrow(policy[i][j]);
      cells.push(html`<div style="background:${colour}; width:80px; height:80px; display:flex; flex-direction:column; align-items:center; justify-content:center; font-family:monospace; border:1px solid #999;">
        <div style="font-size: 0.85rem;">${v[i][j].toFixed(2)}</div>
        <div style="font-size: 1.4rem;">${sym}</div>
      </div>`);
    }
  }
  return html`<div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(${N}, 80px); gap:0; width:max-content; margin:1rem auto;">${cells}</div>
  <div style="text-align:center; font-size:0.9rem; color:#666;">PI iteration ${piIter}: cell shows v_¤Ç(s); arrow shows current ¤Ç(s).</div>`;
}
```

Watch how each iteration produces a *much* bigger jump in the policy than VI
does. By PI iteration 3 or 4, the policy is already optimal. PI converges
in *finitely many steps* ÔÇö not asymptotically.

## Wonder

1. PI does *exact* policy evaluation at each step ÔÇö that's much more work
   per iteration than VI's single sweep. Yet PI converges in just a handful
   of iterations. Where does the work-per-iteration go?
2. After improving $\pi$ greedily w.r.t.\ $v_\pi$, why does the new policy
   $\pi'$ have $v_{\pi'} \ge v_\pi$ pointwise? Could greedy improvement
   ever *hurt*?
3. There are only finitely many deterministic policies. Could PI cycle
   between two of them?

## Discover

### Problem 1 ÔÇö Solve $v_\pi$ for a fixed policy

Take the 3├ù3 grid with the trivial policy $\pi_0(s) = $ "up" everywhere.
What is $v_{\pi_0}(0, 0)$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ÔÇö a nudge</summary>
Under "always up," $(0, 0)$ keeps bumping into the top wall. Where do you
end up? What's the discounted return?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(0, 0)$ tries to go up but is blocked: stays at $(0, 0)$. Reward 0
forever. $v_{\pi_0}(0, 0) = 0$. Same for any cell that, under "up", never
reaches the goal: most of the grid. Only the goal $(2, 2)$ has $v_{\pi_0}(2, 2) = 1/(1 - \gamma) = 10$
(absorbing self-loop with reward 1 each step).

**A bad policy gives bad values.** That's exactly the diagnosis PI's
improvement step uses.
</details>

### Problem 2 ÔÇö Greedy improvement

From $v_{\pi_0}$ above, compute the greedy policy $\pi_1$. Which states
get a different action than $\pi_0$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ÔÇö a nudge</summary>
$\pi_1(s) = \arg\max_a [r(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' | s, a) v_{\pi_0}(s')]$.
For most $s$, $v_{\pi_0}(s') = 0$, so the $\arg\max$ picks the action with
highest *immediate* reward.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Most cells have $v_{\pi_0} = 0$. So greedy improvement at, say, $(1, 2)$:
- "up" ÔåÆ $(0, 2)$, $r = 0$, $q = 0$.
- "down" ÔåÆ $(2, 2)$, $r = 1$, $q = 1 + 0.9 \cdot 10 = 10$. ÔåÉ winner!
- "left" ÔåÆ $(1, 1)$, $q = 0$. "right" ÔåÆ blocked, stays, $q = 0$.

So $\pi_1(1, 2) = $ "down." Similarly $\pi_1(2, 1) = $ "right." Both
adjacent-to-goal cells now correctly point at the goal. **One step of
greedy improvement fixes two cells.**

For cells far from the goal (e.g.\ $(0, 0)$), all $q$-values are still 0;
the greedy choice is arbitrary. PI's *next* evaluation will fix them.
</details>

### Problem 3 ÔÇö The policy improvement theorem

Suppose $\pi'$ is greedy w.r.t.\ $v_\pi$. Show that $v_{\pi'}(s) \ge v_\pi(s)$
for **every** state $s$. (This is the engine of PI.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ÔÇö a nudge</summary>
By the greedy choice, $T^* v_\pi (s) \ge T^\pi v_\pi(s) = v_\pi(s)$. Now
iterate the operator and use monotonicity.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 ÔÇö the technique</summary>
$T^{\pi'} v_\pi (s) = T^* v_\pi (s) \ge v_\pi(s)$ pointwise. Apply $T^{\pi'}$
again; it preserves pointwise $\ge$ (monotonicity). Iterate.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Step 1.** By greediness, $T^{\pi'} v_\pi(s) = \max_a [r(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s'|s, a) v_\pi(s')] \ge v_\pi(s)$,
where the last inequality uses $v_\pi = T^\pi v_\pi$ and $T^\pi v_\pi \le T^* v_\pi$
(max ÔëÑ average).

**Step 2 (monotonicity).** $T^{\pi'}$ is monotone: if $u \ge w$ pointwise,
then $T^{\pi'} u \ge T^{\pi'} w$ (sums of non-negative weights).

**Step 3 (iterate).** $v_\pi \le T^{\pi'} v_\pi \le (T^{\pi'})^2 v_\pi \le \cdots \to v_{\pi'}$.

So $v_{\pi'} \ge v_\pi$ pointwise. **Greedy improvement never hurts**, and
strictly improves at least one state unless $\pi$ was already optimal.
</details>

### Problem 4 ÔÇö Termination in finite steps

Use Problem 3 to argue PI terminates in finitely many iterations on a
finite MDP.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ÔÇö a nudge</summary>
There are finitely many deterministic policies ÔÇö at most $|A|^{|S|}$.
Each PI iteration produces a policy with strictly larger $v_\pi$ (or stops).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
On a finite MDP, the set of deterministic policies has size $|A|^{|S|}$.
Each PI iteration produces $\pi_{k+1}$ with $v_{\pi_{k+1}} \ge v_{\pi_k}$
pointwise *and* strictly greater at some state ÔÇö unless $\pi_{k+1} = \pi_k$,
in which case the algorithm has reached a *fixed point of greedy
improvement* and we have $v_{\pi_k} = T^* v_{\pi_k}$, i.e.\ $v_{\pi_k} = v^*$.

So either $v_\pi$ strictly increases (visiting a new policy) or we stop.
Since there are finitely many policies, we stop in at most $|A|^{|S|}$
iterations ÔÇö usually far fewer. **Finite, not asymptotic, convergence.**
</details>

### Problem 5 ÔÇö Compare to VI: number of operations

A VI iteration costs $O(|S|^2 |A|)$ (one sweep). A PI iteration costs
$O(|S|^3)$ (matrix inversion for evaluation) plus $O(|S|^2 |A|)$ (greedy
step). When does PI win?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ÔÇö a nudge</summary>
PI does fewer iterations but each is more expensive. Compare total work:
$N_{\text{PI}} \cdot |S|^3$ vs. $N_{\text{VI}} \cdot |S|^2 |A|$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
PI typically converges in $O(|S|/(1-\gamma))$ iterations or fewer.
VI takes $O(\log(1/\varepsilon)/(1-\gamma))$ iterations to error $\varepsilon$.
For small $|S|$ and $\gamma$ close to 1, PI usually wins on iteration count
but loses per-iteration. For huge state spaces, even one PI iteration is
infeasible (matrix inversion is $O(|S|^3)$). **VI scales; PI converges
fewer-times-but-more-thoroughly.** Truncated PI (Lesson 15) bridges them.
</details>

::: {.key-insight}
PI's structure ÔÇö *evaluate-then-improve* ÔÇö is the template for every RL
algorithm that follows. Replace "exact evaluation" with "MC sample
evaluation" ÔåÆ MC Basic. Replace it with "TD update" ÔåÆ SARSA / Q-learning.
The architecture stays; only the evaluation step changes.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Policy Iteration (PI).** Initialise any deterministic policy $\pi_0$.
Repeat for $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$:

1. **Policy evaluation:** solve the Bellman equation
   $$v_{\pi_k} = r_{\pi_k} + \gamma P_{\pi_k} v_{\pi_k}, \quad \text{i.e.\ } v_{\pi_k} = (I - \gamma P_{\pi_k})^{-1} r_{\pi_k},$$
   for $v_{\pi_k}$.
2. **[Policy improvement](/glossary.qmd#policy-improvement):**
   $$\pi_{k+1}(s) \leftarrow \arg\max_a\!\Big[r(s, a) + \gamma\!\sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)\,v_{\pi_k}(s')\Big].$$

Stop when $\pi_{k+1} = \pi_k$. Return $\pi^* = \pi_k$ and $v^* = v_{\pi_k}$.

**Theorem (Howard, 1960).** PI on a finite discounted MDP terminates in at
most $|A|^{|S|}$ iterations with the optimal policy and value.

:::

A verse:

> *Evaluate the policy you have;*
> *Improve by going greedy on it.*
> *No step makes you worse,*
> *And finite policies must run out.*

## Apply

### Worked example ÔÇö PI on a 2-state MDP

States $\{s_1, s_2\}$, two actions per state, $\gamma = 0.5$.

| State | Action | Reward | Next state |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $s_1$ | $a$ | 0 | $s_1$ |
| $s_1$ | $b$ | 1 | $s_2$ |
| $s_2$ | $a$ | 0 | $s_2$ |
| $s_2$ | $b$ | 2 | $s_1$ |

**$\pi_0(s_1) = a, \pi_0(s_2) = a$** (both "stay").

**Iter 1 ÔÇö Eval.** Both states stay: zero reward forever.
$v_{\pi_0}(s_1) = v_{\pi_0}(s_2) = 0$.

**Iter 1 ÔÇö Improve.** $q_{\pi_0}(s_1, a) = 0, q_{\pi_0}(s_1, b) = 1 + 0.5 \cdot 0 = 1$. So $\pi_1(s_1) = b$.
$q_{\pi_0}(s_2, a) = 0, q_{\pi_0}(s_2, b) = 2 + 0.5 \cdot 0 = 2$. So $\pi_1(s_2) = b$.
**$\pi_1 = (b, b)$**.

**Iter 2 ÔÇö Eval.** Under $\pi_1$, $s_1 \xrightarrow{b} s_2$ (reward 1),
$s_2 \xrightarrow{b} s_1$ (reward 2). Set up Bellman:
$$v(s_1) = 1 + 0.5 v(s_2), \quad v(s_2) = 2 + 0.5 v(s_1).$$
Substitute: $v(s_1) = 1 + 0.5(2 + 0.5 v(s_1)) = 2 + 0.25 v(s_1) \Rightarrow v(s_1) = 8/3 \approx 2.67$.
$v(s_2) = 2 + 0.5 \cdot 8/3 = 10/3 \approx 3.33$.

**Iter 2 ÔÇö Improve.** $q(s_1, a) = 0 + 0.5 \cdot 8/3 = 4/3$. $q(s_1, b) = 1 + 0.5 \cdot 10/3 = 8/3$. So $\pi_2(s_1) = b$ ÔÇö *unchanged*.
$q(s_2, a) = 0 + 0.5 \cdot 10/3 = 5/3$. $q(s_2, b) = 2 + 0.5 \cdot 8/3 = 10/3$. So $\pi_2(s_2) = b$ ÔÇö *unchanged*.

**$\pi_2 = \pi_1$ ÔçÆ stop.** $\pi^* = (b, b)$, $v^* = (8/3, 10/3)$. **Two
iterations.**

### Practice ÔÇö Warm

**W1.** Verify the Bellman equations $v^* = (8/3, 10/3)$ from the worked
example by direct substitution into the BOE.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$v^*(s_1) = \max(0 + 0.5 \cdot 8/3,\ 1 + 0.5 \cdot 10/3) = \max(4/3, 8/3) = 8/3$. Ô£ô
$v^*(s_2) = \max(0 + 0.5 \cdot 10/3,\ 2 + 0.5 \cdot 8/3) = \max(5/3, 10/3) = 10/3$. Ô£ô
</details>

**W2.** What if we'd started with $\pi_0 = (b, b)$ in the worked example?
How many iterations would PI need?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\pi_0 = (b, b)$ is already optimal. Iter 1 evaluates and finds $v^* = (8/3, 10/3)$,
then greedy improvement returns the same policy. **One iteration.** PI is
sensitive to initial policy in the *iteration count*, but converges to the
same $\pi^*$ either way.
</details>

**W3.** State why "$v_\pi = (I - \gamma P_\pi)^{-1} r_\pi$" is a *closed-form*
solution rather than another iteration.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The Bellman equation for $v_\pi$ is *linear*: $v = r + \gamma P_\pi v$.
Rearranging: $(I - \gamma P_\pi) v = r$, then $v = (I - \gamma P_\pi)^{-1} r$.
The matrix $I - \gamma P_\pi$ is invertible because all eigenvalues of
$\gamma P_\pi$ are $\le \gamma < 1$. So $v_\pi$ is computed by *one*
linear system solve, not iteration. (For large $|S|$, iterative solvers
beat direct inversion; that's the trick behind truncated PI.)
</details>

### Practice ÔÇö Working

**P1.** PI on a 3-state MDP. States $\{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$, two actions per
state, $\gamma = 0.5$. Rewards/transitions:

| s | a | r | s' |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $s_1$ | $a$ | 0 | $s_1$ |
| $s_1$ | $b$ | 0 | $s_2$ |
| $s_2$ | $a$ | 0 | $s_2$ |
| $s_2$ | $b$ | 0 | $s_3$ |
| $s_3$ | $a$ | 1 | $s_3$ |
| $s_3$ | $b$ | 1 | $s_3$ |

Start with $\pi_0 = a$ everywhere. Run PI to optimality.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ÔÇö a nudge</summary>
$\pi_0$ has every state stuck. Only $s_3$ accumulates reward.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Iter 1 eval.** $v_{\pi_0}(s_3) = 1/(1 - 0.5) = 2$. Others 0.
**Iter 1 improve.** $s_2$: $q(b) = 0 + 0.5 \cdot 2 = 1 > q(a) = 0$. So $\pi_1(s_2) = b$. $\pi_1(s_1)$ still arbitrary (both give 0). Take $a$ (no change).
**Iter 2 eval.** $\pi_1 = (a, b, *)$. $v_{\pi_1}(s_3) = 2$, $v_{\pi_1}(s_2) = 0 + 0.5 \cdot 2 = 1$, $v_{\pi_1}(s_1) = 0$.
**Iter 2 improve.** $s_1$: $q(b) = 0 + 0.5 \cdot 1 = 0.5 > 0 = q(a)$. So $\pi_2(s_1) = b$.
**Iter 3 eval.** $\pi_2 = (b, b, *)$. $v(s_1) = 0.5 \cdot 1 = 0.5, v(s_2) = 1, v(s_3) = 2$.
**Iter 3 improve.** No changes. **Stop after 3 iterations.** Optimal:
"chain to $s_3$ as fast as possible." Information propagates one state
per iteration, just like in VI ÔÇö but here through *exact* evaluation.
</details>

**P2.** Show that if $\pi_{k+1} = \pi_k$ in PI, then $v_{\pi_k} = v^*$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ÔÇö a nudge</summary>
$\pi_{k+1} = \pi_k$ means greedy w.r.t.\ $v_{\pi_k}$ gives back $\pi_k$.
Translate that into operators.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\pi_{k+1} = \pi_k$ means $T^* v_{\pi_k} = T^{\pi_k} v_{\pi_k} = v_{\pi_k}$.
So $v_{\pi_k}$ is a fixed point of $T^*$. By Lesson 12 the fixed point of
$T^*$ is unique, so $v_{\pi_k} = v^*$ and $\pi_k$ is optimal. **The PI
stopping criterion ($\pi$ stops changing) is *exactly* "we've reached the
unique fixed point of $T^*$".**
</details>

**P3.** Modify P1: add an action $c$ at $s_1$ giving reward $-10$ and
staying at $s_1$. Does this affect $\pi^*$ or any $v_\pi$ in PI?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\pi^*$ is unchanged: $c$ is dominated by $b$ at every iteration. $v_\pi$
also unchanged for any $\pi$ that doesn't *select* $c$. **Adding strictly
worse actions doesn't change PI's trajectory** ÔÇö improvement is greedy and
ignores them. (This robustness is also why we don't need to enumerate all
policies; PI navigates the policy graph directly.)
</details>

### Practice ÔÇö Stretch

**S1.** *Generalised policy iteration.* Suppose at each step you only
*partially* evaluate $v_\pi$ ÔÇö say, $K$ iterations of $T^\pi$. Show that
the greedy improvement still produces an improved policy *if $K$ is large
enough*. (How large?)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ÔÇö a nudge</summary>
$\hat v$ approximates $v_\pi$ within $\gamma^K \|\hat v_0 - v_\pi\|_\infty$.
For greedy w.r.t.\ $\hat v$ to match greedy w.r.t.\ $v_\pi$, recall S1 of
Lesson 13: action gaps must dominate the approximation error.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
After $K$ Bellman steps, $\|\hat v - v_\pi\|_\infty \le \gamma^K \|v_0 - v_\pi\|_\infty$.
By the same argument as VI, the greedy policy w.r.t.\ $\hat v$ matches that
w.r.t.\ $v_\pi$ once this error falls below $\Delta_{\min}/(2\gamma)$ where
$\Delta_{\min}$ is the smallest action gap. Solve for $K$:
$K \ge \log(2\gamma\,\|v_0 - v_\pi\|_\infty / \Delta_{\min}) / \log(1/\gamma)$.
**This is the seed of truncated PI (next lesson).**
</details>

**S2.** Show that PI is *equivalent to Newton's method* for finding the
fixed point of $T^*$ when $|A| = 1$ (single action). What's the analogy
when $|A| > 1$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ÔÇö a nudge</summary>
With $|A| = 1$, $T^* = T^\pi$ is linear. PI evaluates *exactly*, which is
equivalent to inverting the Jacobian ÔÇö Newton's step.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
With $|A| = 1$, the BOE is a linear equation $v = T^\pi v$ and PI solves it
in one shot via $(I - \gamma P_\pi)^{-1} r_\pi$ ÔÇö which is Newton's method
applied to $F(v) = v - T^\pi v$ at $v_0$. The Jacobian $F'(v) = I - \gamma P_\pi$
is constant (linear case), so Newton converges in one step. With $|A| > 1$,
$T^*$ is *piecewise* linear (different "max" branches over different
regions); PI is Newton's method *for piecewise-linear systems*: each step
solves the current linear piece exactly, then the greedy improvement picks
the next piece. This is Pollatschek-Avi-Itzhak / Puterman-Brumelle's
deeper insight (1980s) ÔÇö it's why PI converges *quadratically* in the
non-degenerate case.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
Ronald Howard's 1960 book *Dynamic Programming and Markov Processes*
introduced PI and proved finite-step convergence. The result was striking:
VI's geometric convergence is *asymptotic*, but PI hits the optimum
exactly in finitely many steps. Howard also identified that real-world
operations problems ÔÇö equipment replacement, inventory control ÔÇö needed
just a handful of PI iterations, even for hundreds of states.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"PI requires the matrix $P$ to be small enough to invert."** Strictly,
   yes ÔÇö but iterative inner solves (Lesson 15) bypass this. The
   *conceptual* picture stays exact-eval-then-improve.
2. **"PI's policy can oscillate."** It cannot. Each iteration produces a
   policy with strictly higher $v_\pi$ (or stops). No cycles possible.
3. **"VI is just slow PI."** Almost true: VI is $K = 1$ truncated PI
   (Lesson 15). They sit on a continuum.
4. **"You need an *initial* good policy."** No ÔÇö PI converges from any
   starting policy. Bad starts just take a few more iterations.
:::

### Takeaways

1. **PI alternates exact policy evaluation with greedy improvement.**
2. **Policy improvement theorem.** Greedy w.r.t.\ $v_\pi$ produces $\pi'$
   with $v_{\pi'} \ge v_\pi$ pointwise ÔÇö *never* worse.
3. **Finite termination** in $\le |A|^{|S|}$ iterations, almost always
   far fewer in practice.
4. **PI Ôëê Newton on the BOE.** That's why each step is so powerful.

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you state the policy improvement theorem and sketch its proof?
2. Did you do the worked example by hand before reading it?
3. Can you explain *why* PI must terminate in finite steps?

- **3 yes:** move on to Lesson 15 (truncated PI).
- **2 yes:** redo P2 ÔÇö it's the cleanest "why does PI converge" argument.
- **0ÔÇô1 yes:** re-read *Discover*, especially Problem 3.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **Lesson 13 (VI).** Provides the BOE-iteration backbone and the greedy
  extraction step.
- **Lesson 7 (Bellman matrix form).** Supplies the closed-form
  $v_\pi = (I - \gamma P_\pi)^{-1} r_\pi$.

### What this unlocks

- **Lesson 15 ÔÇö Truncated PI.** PI ($K = \infty$) and VI ($K = 1$) are
  endpoints of a single algorithm.
- **All MC and TD methods (Lessons 17-26).** They replace "exact
  policy evaluation" with sampled estimates ÔÇö but keep PI's
  evaluate-improve loop intact.
- **Actor-critic methods (Lessons 35-37).** "Critic" estimates $v_\pi$;
  "actor" improves $\pi$. Same architecture as PI, with function
  approximation.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Every actor-critic method (A2C, PPO, SAC) is a "soft
generalised PI" ÔÇö alternate critic-fit (Ôëê evaluation) and actor-update
(Ôëê improvement). Howard's 1960 architecture, scaled to deep nets.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Operations research.** PI was the workhorse of MDP-based decision
support in operations and engineering for decades ÔÇö equipment
replacement, inventory, queueing, even reservoir management. It still is,
when state spaces are tractable.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
gives the single-paragraph view of reinforcement learning. PI's
evaluate-improve loop is "policy iteration" in that view.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- Source: Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement Learning*
  (Springer 2025), ┬º4.2. Cite freely; never transcribe.
- Howard's 1960 framing for the algorithm and termination theorem.
- Touchstones: Sutton & Barto (the iconic loop diagram), Puterman 1994
  (the Newton's-method connection).
:::
